sentences.text: I remember Purim.
It was a nice-- it was a very happy holiday.
I remember being in the synagogue.
And I think we didn't belong to the main synagogue.
It was a small synagogue because I remember only one room.
But that-- this could have been in the ghetto already though.
I'm not sure.
It probably was.
And I remember them dancing with the Torah and apples and flags.
Like these flags--
Right.
Yeah, that's what I remember.
Well, my-- my next-- my brother, Isacz.
Yeah.
My brother.
Isacz.
I, I-- you know, he-- I remember him very well because, fortunately, he, he did survive the war.
And but in pre-war, what I remember about him is that he, he was in the Polish armed forces.
He-- well, everybody had to-- had to go in for two years, but lots of people got out of it.
But he wanted to go.
He was very patriotic and felt it was his duty to serve Poland because he was a citizen of Poland.
And he wanted all his rights and privileges, so he served for two years in the army, Polish army, under very difficult conditions because it was terrible anti-Semitism.
And although he could've qualified to go to cadet school, officer school, you know, there was no question of him going.
But he-- because there were so few educated Poles in the army, he was like an aide to the colonel.
He was his clerk.
You know, he did the writing and things like that.
So I remember him in his uniform.
He used to come home on leave in his uniform.
And he had a big dagger hanging down, down the side.
And he used to walk me to my school, and I felt very proud about that.
He was in, in business with my father, and the two of them always fought something horribly.
You know, it was always arguments.
My brother could do nothing right.
If he bought-- if he bought a load of timber for half the market price, it was too much.
[LAUGHS] But that was very typical of the time.
But you know, but behind it, my father adored him and would say all kinds of nice things about him.
But I wanted to be part of the act too.
As soon as possible, I wanted to-- to take my, my place in the family.
And I just remember him before the war as having a lot of friends.
He was very active in the Zionist movement.
He, he was-- also had some Polish friends, some Christian friends too.
Yeah, they seemed to.
And you know, they came to our house.
And I also remember him staying up nights and playing.
He was a little bit of a good, good time Charlie.
You know, he used to like to play cards and drink whiskey I think.
In those days, anyway.
17 I think.
About 17 years older, yeah.
And that-- that's about all the memories I have of, of Izzy before the war.
Then the other--
No.
No, the only one that got married in our family was Ronia.
Then my other sister, Lola, she-- I didn't like her very much, you know?
Because she kind of always seemed to criticize me.
She always said the other little kids behaved better, and they had better manners.
She was really a snob, still is.
And you know, she was very conscious of, of class, and you know, who they were.
And she wanted to be better, and she was kind of unhappy I think.
I asked my brother that question, and he didn't-- he seemed to think not.
He seemed to think not.
I think maybe Izzy and, and Ronia were close, but not Lola.
I think she was like three years old when her mother died.
And--
Right.
Frania, Ronia, and Lola were born by my aunt, and I'll explain that.
My father was married, and his wife died.
And it was the custom-- in those days, it was even the law-- that if there was an unmarried woman in the house still available-- that you would marry that woman.
And that's what my father did.
He married his first wife's sister, because my mother was one of six girls and one boy.
So that's what happened, someplace around 19-- I don't know-- '22, '23 I think.
She came in, married my father, and, and there were already three children, three very young children.
It was a Jewish law.
I don't know if it's a law or if it was a custom.
I think it was a law, you know, rabbinical law.
You know, you had to-- you know, like the widow or something like that.
I'm not sure that she was.
I think she was.
I think she was, but I'm not sure.
I know-- I remember she was in-- she was engaged to be married around 1937-38.
And I remember there was a lot of negotiation going on with the other family.
It was very dramatic.
And my brother had to travel, it's a long distance, to the other family, appraise them.
And then they had to, I guess, agree on a price.
And I thought they were going to give away all my inheritance and I would have nothing left when I grew up.
[LAUGHTER]
No.
I think the war came in, or something happened.
Yeah.
Yeah, well, when you married-- when you're married off a daughter, there's usually a dowry came with it.
And that was part of the negotiation.
Yeah.
But I think that they met on their own.
There was no third party involved in that.
Yeah.
She died when she was 13.
And she was, again, from the first mother, from my father's first wife.
And I had this recollection of walking one day with my father.
We went from one of his customers or something like that.
And he was telling me how this young girl had died and how sad it had made him, how very unhappy he was.
And I had this memory, and I didn't know whether it was real or not.
Sometimes you just don't trust, you know, you don't know what's real or what isn't.
So I checked it with my brother, and he never talked about her.
But sure enough, there was another child, and she died of rheumatic fever, I believe.
And she-- they had all the specialists, the doctors, everything and--
Yeah.
Yeah.
Mhm.
Well, she was my-- you know, my 100% sister.
I remember her as a teenager, a very happy teenager with lots of friends and very kind of gay and happy.
Just a typical teenager, you know, full of life.
And just that we spent a lot of the holidays together in the summertime.
And that's about all I remember of her.
I don't remember.
Yeah, definitely.
Yeah.
She was like my mother really.
My father.
My father was very important to me.
You know, I always thought that I was his-- because I was a boy and I was the youngest one-- I thought I was his like very favorite.
I was like his favorite.
And-- but he was considerably older at the time than other, other kids' father, because he had me so late.
I didn't like that too much.
But he was a very energetic person, very-- terribly hard working.
And you know, and also, I think very kind of nervous.
Well not maybe nervous, but very excitable.
Very excitable.
He had a lot-- there was a lot of frustrations in his life, being in business in Poland and so forth.
And he would-- he was a hollerer.
He would shout a lot, never at me, but he would rage.
Yeah.
Well, it was just terrible frustrating to do business in Poland, because there were so many obstacle to being Jewish.
You know, you had to bribe so many people, and you had to please so many people.
And it was just a very frustrating experience for him.
Not to my knowledge.
Not to my-- I'm sure there was a lot of thinking about that, but again, I-- I'm not aware of it as a child.
Post-war, I would say, you know, from when I talked to my brother, I would say not really.
They didn't feel like they had an alternative.
Well, they felt the war is going to be very imminent.
And they--
Very imminent.
Like any day, war would break out.
And they were terribly afraid of what Germany would do to the Jews because they already knew what was happening to the German Jews.
You know, they were expelled from Germany.
And there was a lot of Jews that had-- Polish Jews that had moved to Germany.
All of those Jews were expelled, and Poland wouldn't take him in, you know?
And so they were living like in [INAUDIBLE].
So we had a lot of experience of knowing that something very unpleasant was going to happen.
Mhm.
But they were afraid of the German onslaught.
They wanted to try and see if they could avoid it.

So what I remember very vividly is that one day just a few, maybe a day or so before, or maybe even on the same day that the war was-- well, Germany just invaded Poland because my brother got called up to the army.
And they decided that they were going to go to a little village called Sulejow.
No.
And I know it begins with S-z-y-l-v-i-a, something like that.
It's in my-- it's in my story.
And there was, we had family there.
We had-- my father's brother lived there, or just on the outskirt of it.
It was a very small village, maybe just a few thousand inhabitants.
I think it was mostly Jewish.
It was of no consequence, really, strategically.
And I remember that they decided that, let's go.
Let's get out of this Piotrkow because they were afraid it was going to be bombed, and let's go to Sulejow.
So I remember that one day we started on our way, and I don't remember how, whether we had a wagon or horse or whatever.
Oh, I can't remember walking even.
But we, we started up that way.
And on the way-- it was-- I felt really gay, you know?
I felt like it was a holiday.
It was kind of exciting.
On the way, I think we stopped and we saw my uncle.
I think his name was Bernard.
He had-- he was in the business, where he was making ceramic.
There were ovens there, like into, into the earth there were ovens, and they baked this ceramic or something.
And he was making bricks and things like that.
That was his business.
He quite well off.
And we saw him, and then we went into this town of Sulejow.
And I remember that seems like no sooner did we enter it that we heard airplanes.
And almost immediately these, these-- there were like dive bombers that would come down.
And they were starting bombing everything, dropping bombs.
Right where I was standing.
And, and then it--
Yeah.
Right.
Yeah, I think it's in the history book.
And then, after the bombers would be-- would come the fighters.
And they would fire the machine guns and everything.
Fire started immediately.
I think it was mostly all wooden construction.
And I was standing up and I was looking at the plane.
I was just fascinated by the plane.
And those planes were flying so low that I could actually see the, the pilot in the cockpit.
And these-- everybody-- people were running away, trying to get into, dive into some kind of a cover, especially those ovens.
And you could actually see the, the fighter planes just following them, and chasing, and firing their guns at them.
So there was just total, total chaos.
And someplace, at a point like that,  I'm standing there, and I'm looking up at those airplanes.
And my mother fell on top of me.
She knocked me down to the ground.
And I stayed on the ground I don't know how long.
All I remember was there was a coal, like a coal place next to us, a place that had coal.
It was terrible screaming, and there were-- and I-- you could see parts of bodies being strewn in the air.
And horses were-- you know, were were dead on the street.
And mostly I remember the smell, the smell of, of human flesh being burned.
Yeah.
Yeah.
It was just, just awful.
Yeah.
That you never forget, that, that smell.
And somehow or other, I don't-- I have a lapse of memory, is that the next thing I know is that I am-- I think I'm with my father.
And we're walking through this, this village looking for Mother.
And there are corpses everywhere, and we're looking at the corpses.
And we were afraid that the planes were going to come back again, which indeed they did.
So we ran into the forest.
Poland has a lot of forests.
And--
Yeah, it could have been, could have been my sister too.
I really don't-- I don't remember anybody but me and my father.
I know my brother was away in the army.
He was on the German front.
And I know, when I look back in the history books, there were over a 1,000 people killed in that massacre, which it was, because it was totally defenseless.
There was no--
Yeah.
Because the streets-- well, see, I failed to mention that we weren't the only ones that had this idea of leaving Piotrkow.
The entire-- when we hit the road, the entire Jewish community was on their way.
I think entire.
So this is why this little village was so jammed with people.
It was like being in a football stadium or something.
It was just totally wall-to-wall people.
So this is why there were such huge casualties there.
So, so the Jewish people knew even that early, first day of the war, that they were one of the main targets?
Oh yeah.
Well, Hitler made it quite clear, quite clear that Jews were a problem, and he would find, find a solution for it.
We're in the forest maybe, I don't know, three or four days, maybe five days.
I know we sleeping on the ground.
We see part of the Polish army there.
They're there too.
They looked very unhappy, not very friendly at all.
I mean, like, they didn't want us anywhere near them.
And I remember that--
What?
I don't know, it was either that they were anti-Semitic or-- probably.
I would say that was the reason.
They didn't want to have no part of Jews.
And yet, you know, there's 30,000 Jewish soldiers in the Polish army, wearing the Polish uniform, that died.
It's way out of proportion, you know?
Anyhow, I was very hungry, slept on the ground.
I remember us going to a peasant house, and the lady served us, she either gave it to us, or we paid her for it, some potatoes and sauerkraut.
And those tasted wonderful, and that was wonderful.
Probably my most favorite meal to this day is potatoes and sauerkraut.
No.
No, that was Polish.
Peasant woman.
And so I don't--
Yeah.
I remember that because I was so terribly hungry.
And, and she gave me something to eat.
So that was wonderful.
And--
No.
No, absolutely not.
I was just--
We leave.
Finally, we leave the forest.
We go back into the village of Sulejow.
And my father is still trying to find out what happened to Mother.
And he's talking to people, and some people say that, oh yes, we saw her get on a wagon.
And somebody gave her a ride, and she moved to another town.
I don't know.
I can't answer that.
I don't know.
I just don't know.
Then we go back to Piotrkow.
And in Piotrkow, we pass by the apartment building that we lived in.
And we see that one wing of the building was bombed.
I would say probably about a week.
So, so obviously, we don't have a place to stay.
So we go back to my Aunt Ruth Rolnik, and we stay with them.
And they put me to bed, and I couldn't sleep in bed because it was too soft.
So I went and slept on the floor because I was used to sleeping on the hard surface.
Yeah.
She was in a house.
She lived in a house.
And my two cousins were there, and my Uncle Rolnik was there.
Yeah.
And while we were there, the Germans had already come in to, to-- into Piotrkow.
They already had taken over Piotrkow.
Oh, probably for a little while.
I don't think for too long, maybe a few weeks or so.
I don't remember.
I don't remember anything about it.
Yeah.
I don't remember what it was like.
I-- The next memories I really have is that there is a ghetto established.
I know that we cannot live in the same part of town as we lived before.
And so we find, I think my father was able to find a room, one room I think and a kitchen.
In the ghetto.
Yeah.
And we-- the regulations come in right away.
We had to wear a yellow star and a white arm band with a-- with a Star of David saying we were Jewish.
We could not-- we could not leave the ghetto.
At first, we were able to leave outside the ghetto, and then it was restricted where we couldn't, or certain hours.
But the ghetto, the ghetto in Piotrkow was the first ghetto established by the Germans in Poland.
But it was not a fenced in ghetto like later on Warsaw was.
It was just a restricted area section of town where Jews could live.
And even within that section of town, there were Poles and some Germans that lived there.
Well, not Deutsche, volks Deutsche, Germans of-- or Poles that were of German origin.
It was kind of strange, but I remember they lived in the same houses we did.
And obviously, there was no school anymore.
All--
We were living in a house with the one room, like one room, one and a half room.
And I think at that point there is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think at that point, I think my sister is there with me.
And my mother.
Yeah, my mother had returned.
She had gone to-- she had gone to Warsaw.
I don't know how.
I just don't understand the whole thing to this day, how she could have just--
No, with my sister, but she went by herself I think.
She just took off.
And I don't know how, why.
I don't know.
Who knows?
You know, there was so much going on at the time.
And-- but as a kid, I don't feel myself being too affected.
I'm still wandering around the town.
You know, I remember me and another guy go through town picking up cigarette butts.
We're looking for cigarette butts, collect cigarette butts, because we could take the tobacco out of it, and we could get some money for it.
I have, I have no memory of my mother, outside of the one in Sulejow when she throws herself on top of me.
And I have some glimpses of a memory of her living with us in the ghetto, and her going to the bathroom.
That's the only memory I have of my mother.
I have not been able to restore any memory.
I thought I was much younger then, but actually, my mother lived with me till, till, I was probably about, oh, at least 10 or 11.
And so, that would-- I would have, normally, I would have memories of my mother, but I have no memories of my mother.
Blank.
And she's not there.
Yeah.
Well, you know, there were-- every few weeks, or maybe every day, there was new proclamations by the German authorities.
You couldn't own property.
You could only have so much money on you.
You couldn't do business with the Christians.
Like, if you're a Jewish doctor, you couldn't treat Christians.
And what I remember is that there was-- that the setting up of a Jewish council.
I actually remember that, outside of reading it in the book, that there was.
There were people who-- within the ghetto, Jewish people, generally the people who had been the leaders of the community before-- were in charge of the ghetto.
And they are the ones who dealt with the Germans.
All the orders from the Germans came through the Germans to the Judens-- something Judens, Strauss or something.
Jewish council.
Yeah, or Jewish elders.
And then, that's, that's when-- you know, that's when-- and what they would do is it would be like-- you know, they would impose a fine, so many million slaughters or something like that.
And then, it was up to the council to collect the money and turn it over.
They would force laborers to come in, you know, to have people send them to labor camps and things.
It was up to the Jewish council to do-- they, they basically, they did most of the-- most of the dirty work.
There were, per se, there were not too many Germans in the ghetto in those years, as such.
Mostly there were Polish policemen.
He had come back in about-- I would think they were probably 1940, yeah.
He came back when the ghetto was established.
We were living already in the ghetto.
He was wounded in the war, quite severely.
And he really, they were going to shoot him, but he talked German to them.
Knowing how to speak German saved his life.
Because he was-- when they were going to shoot him, he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], volks Deutsche.
I'm a German.
And because he spoke German so well, they believed him.
And they took him off to the hospital, and he spent like, six months or something in the hospital.
He was burned.
He had wounds in his leg.
And he came back into the ghetto where we were living in the ghetto.
And the first day he came back, you know, we had no expectation that when he would arrive.
And he came in, and we were all absolutely thrilled and--
Yes.
Yes, he-- there was a mail.
Yeah.
He was able to write to us.
He was very fortunate because a lot of the-- a lot of the prisoners of war, even the ones that were treated in the hospital, they would be officially discharged.
And then, they would be machine gunned because they were no longer-- they were no longer prisoners of war, so they had nothing to protect them.
In the end, they knew, yeah, they knew he was Jewish.
And wherever there was no Nazi there present, they were really very human towards him and did their best for him.
When he came back into the ghetto, you know, we didn't know if he was coming back, and there was, of course, my father, and mother, and sister were, we were just so thrilled to see him back.
And it was a big celebration.
And he-- because he was a prisoner of war and he needed medical attention, he was able to go out.
He had permission to go outside the ghetto for treatment.
So he made-- he was able to make contact outside of the ghetto, which was enormously helpful later on.
He was able to establish contact with his former business partners, things like that.
And, I need a rest.
